title: Penne all'Arrabbiata
subtitle: Angry tomato sauce with chilli, garlic, and flat-leaf parsley
description: Arrabbiata — 'angry' in Italian — is a short-cooked, fiery tomato sauce from Rome. It is the puttanesca's simpler cousin: just tomatoes, garlic, dried chilli, and olive oil, finished with flat-leaf parsley. The heat level is the point. A proper arrabbiata should make the back of the throat warm; adjust the chilli to your preference but do not reduce it to nothing. The sauce takes fifteen minutes from start to finish.
sourceNotes: A Roman pasta dish first documented in print in the early twentieth century. Ada Boni's Il Talismano della Felicità (1927) includes an early version; the dish is now a staple of Roman trattoria menus and home cooking across Italy.

glossaryTerms.definition: Italian for 'angry'. In cooking it refers to a style of tomato sauce made fiery with dried chilli. The name reflects the heat of the dish rather than its simplicity.

ingredients.notes: Adjust to preference — this quantity is medium-hot
Half for cooking, half to finish
Rigate (ridged) catches more sauce than smooth penne

steps: Bring a large pot of well-salted water to the boil.

Warm half the olive oil in a medium saucepan over a medium heat. Add the garlic slices and chilli flakes and cook for 1–2 minutes until the garlic is golden at the edges — watch closely, it burns quickly.
Add the crushed tomatoes with their juice. Season with salt, stir, and simmer uncovered for 12–15 minutes until thickened.
Cook the penne in the boiling water until 1 minute short of the packet time. Reserve a ladle of pasta water, then drain.
Add the penne to the sauce with a splash of pasta water. Toss over a medium heat for 1 minute until the sauce clings to the ridges.
Remove from the heat. Drizzle over the remaining olive oil, scatter with parsley, toss, and serve.

content.text: The ridged tubes of penne rigate are not an accident — the ridges hold the thick,

sauce in a way that smooth penne does not. Use rigate if you can find it. The 1 tsp of chilli gives a confident heat; reduce to half a teaspoon for a gentler version, or increase for something properly fierce.

Set the pasta water on to boil. Warm half of {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} of olive oil in a medium saucepan over a medium heat. Add the sliced garlic and the dried chilli flakes. Cook for 1–2 minutes, watching constantly — the garlic should be golden at the edges, not brown. Add the crushed tomatoes with all their juice and the salt. Simmer uncovered for 12–15 minutes until the sauce has thickened.

Cook {{penne-rigate}} of penne until 1 minute short of the packet time. Reserve a ladle of pasta water, then drain. Add the penne to the sauce with a splash of pasta water and toss over a medium heat for 1 minute. Remove from the heat, drizzle over the remaining olive oil, add the parsley, toss, and serve.
